speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Okay, I guess it's gonna stay this way. All righty, now let's go back to the train ride. Coming out under six, you know, we rode free on the train. Well, I was under six. I was in the first of October and it came out in August, I guess it was. So Dad was passing Archie and I off. Archie was two years older than I. He was passing us off at twins. He had an awful argument with the conductor. The conductor looked at it, pointed at Archie, and he said, Oh, he's sick. He isn't sick. I don't know, but he said, this fella, he's quite a beast, and he's eight. It was just the opposite way. But I was the biggest of the two, you see. I was bigger than he was. I will remember that as long as I live. He swore up and down, but I was ill. But I don't remember too much about it. You know, we got in the Spokane and out here. And then Ed had come ahead and he'd had all the cattle. He'd come in the freight train. And he had all the cattle wagon unloaded and all the stuff up to the place. And then when we come in, he met us.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: There was already a house then?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. Dad bought a place up here. It was four miles north. And I know us kids, we walked along and we met us in the wagon. And we'd go walking along behind the wagon, and we was peeking out through the woods looking for Indians. We did, though. We'd go and get a lot of Indians out here. We were looking for them. That's just about all I remember, the trip. I remember too much.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, how old was Ed then?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, let's see.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Sixty -nine and eighty -three, he was fourteen years old. I was six. Ed was about twenty -twenty. He was a young man then. Yeah, twenty or twenty -one, something like that. But I was trying to think of it. Well, at least somebody brought the buggy in and the horse. And Dad and Mother rode out in that. But I know we was in the wagon, brought in the big wagon.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't think we mentioned this was from North Dakota, which you came.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you get to bring a lot of your household things or?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, I shipped everything.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Everything?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: A boxcar loaded with all the household goods. I think everything, the wagon, the buggy, and the household goods, and everything went into one boxcar. Then another cattle car for the horses and the cows, all of that. I thought, oh, Ed'd come out ahead because they had to be watered, fed the cattle. And he'd come right with the freight train, and they got here unloading. Got everything out there when we would say they'd come in and let us. The place was all set up ready.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's pretty nice.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But I don't remember too much of the others. And I don't know whether Hilda and... No, Mabel didn't come. I don't remember whether Hilda did that or not. I kind of think she did. I think she came at the same time. But if you could talk to her, she could probably remember her quite a bit more.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Do you remember what the town looked like when you...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, I know it was a lot of dust, and there was stumps in the streets, board sidewalks. Whatever time it rained, it was mud. It was that way up until 1920, because I remember Ed and I.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You know where the bank is?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There was a lot of stumps out in the street. And they couldn't... people, I guess, were ashamed. They wanted work, but they were ashamed to work out there, grubbing stumps. Ed and I, we spent several days out there grubbing the stumps. They couldn't shoot them, couldn't pull them. We'd dig around, chop the reds out, and get the stumps, and then they'd have somebody haul them out.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, how come they...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You probably remember.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: No, how do they don't?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: So that's the reason why. I'm glad you're here. Oh, yeah, well, I don't know. Remember the stumps. I don't remember how many days we worked there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: How come they couldn't use a stump puller? One in the never room?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, it was the streets in there, and there's no place to anchor. Stumps, you know, you've got to have another stump off quite a way.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, the stumps were in space, right?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They would be over in the side, and most of them were right in that corner, between the service station and the bank, and out into the street there. Oh, I don't know how a stump puller works. Well, a stump puller is... you've got an anchor cable, and it's a big drum, and then a long sweep on it. You hook a team of horses out there, and that day you start going around, and the wines just cable up on the drum. Then there's dogs in it, you know, notches, so that if you want to stop, horses can slack off, stand there and hold it. But there was no way to use it. I don't know whether there was a stump puller in the country there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Not now.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They all had a man. Well, at that time, I don't believe there was too many. I know Dad had to send me to get a new one for pulling stumps up there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We used one. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But, well, there was... yeah, there was... That was in 1920. That must have been in 22, 23, when Edwards and I were doing that work. So you see them streets were... Then they'd haul sodists into some of the worst homes.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you remember that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They'd have sodists, and along the sidewalks, they'd have sodists when it rained, so you wouldn't get muddy. Well, there were some awful mud holes in there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you ever lose anybody in those holes?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, I don't think so. At least you never found anybody there yet. Well, someday, if they start tearing it, they might find some bones and scum.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you remember when they first graveled the streets?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, I remember when they graveled, but I couldn't say when it was. That was a pretty big moment, I imagine. They started. They must have been in graveling. Twenty... well, I think when we was getting them stumps out, it'd be about 22 or 23. They were starting to pick some of them, and there was gravel coming in. And I remember one fourth of July, they'd have a big doings, and there was a man down here. Oh, I don't remember where he'd come from, and that was when they first started this blacktop. Before that, it was all cement. And he had this stuff, and was mixing in the gravel and showing them, trying to sell them stuff to fix the streets with. Oh, he was giving quite a demonstration down there on the fourth. Everybody around them looking, thought how good it was. And you put a match down with the bird. The hot weather, that stuff was a bird. I seen that streets in Moscow at that time. Well, one fourth of July, we were shooting firecrackers. All the ones here were just a fire going all around, and blacktop. Oh, the doggoneers. It was burning, yeah, the kind of a tower. It won't do it now. It's a different material entirely, then. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, they put the gravel on the country road out there when the Sisi camp was out there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What year was that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The Sisi was about... About 33. C. Roosevelt started that when, after he got in office, president, they started the three C camps. And that was one of their projects, fixing up the roads, graveling them. They were all over the country.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The camp was out there, so they scrambled that stretch of road. So they could get the camp.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The rest got the benefit of it, too. Well, that is about the first graveling around out here, too. I see that as the first graveling. I think so. Just about the first. Then they started out on these ridges. They never thought of plowing snow in the wintertime. They never plowed snow. They don't think they started that till getting up pretty close to 1940.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The Sisi boys, they had them out with shovels. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And shoveled it by hand to clear the road from their camp to town.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: In 37, I think it was. Yeah, I believe Art Fiddler told me. In 37, I was working for Driscoll's. And I don't know how many days we never got to mail, so I took the saddle horse and started into town to get to mail. I was working for them. And I got to Fiddler's place, and I left the saddle horse there, then took a pair of skis and come in the rest of the way. We had drifts 10, 12, 15 feet deep up on top of the hill. They get these whole bunch of Sisi boys up there, and they shoveled it out by hand. Well, that was a job.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I'll say.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: How many boys were out there? Do you know how many boys they had out there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't know. They had a slug up. It didn't take them too long after they got started. They had a lot of boys. The road isn't like it is now. There's an old road, and right close to the top there was a cut. It swung around and came up, and there was a deep cut. The cut was about 15 feet deep, I guess, and it was just straight across. You couldn't even ride around besides with a horse because all the snow was too deep. I just took a pair of skis and come into town to get to mail. That kind of thing. Well, that's one of the first things Art said the other day, and he said, I don't think I'd seen him in over 30 years. We got to talk about it. He remembered that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Lending you the skis, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That first year was six feet of snow right on the level. Then that year, 31, we lived out there. It blew and drifted. Everything was shut, so we walked on the snow clear up in the top of the tree. When we got the steps of the car to go through, we looked way up there. We broke a limb to show where the snow had been, and it was way up on the tree. That first year, we was out here in 1912.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Dad had to buy hay, and he bought it over there where the water system is now, the dam, their big meadows. Well, the hay had been cut off of that meadow, and Dad bought hay from Ollie Nelson. No road. Just drove right over the fences and all. It had that much snow. Then it rained and turned around and froze. Horses and all had just walked right on top of the snow over the fence. The cattle was all over. You couldn't bend some in and nothing else. But it made it nice to hold the hay, big loads of hay in the sled.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: In the sled?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. Coming right over the top of the crust. You don't have that winter's year anymore. Not recently. My goodness. Well, you always look for that silver thaw. You remember the silver thaw that was Dad. And that'd be along in, oh, January or February, we'd get a rain. Pretty good rain. And then turn right around and freeze solid. After that, cattle everything would walk right on top of the snow. And that's called a silver thaw. That is a silver thaw, yeah. Rain, and then the sun would come out, be nice and warm, that snow would all... Of course, it would settle. It'd be deep, but we don't even get that much snow now.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You think there's any reason?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There's a reason all right. The timber's gone. Ah. The timber, I think, will bring the rain, and it brings some, I believe. All of these countries, as soon as the timber's gone, the country changes. I don't know why, but I think they find that out. I think that's... I think they found that. I know for rain, I feel. But the timber draws the rain. But I don't know about the snow, but I imagine it'd be the same reason.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We're all covered with timber. Each house was just in an isolated spot, really. And the timber would come right up to the back door, pretty near.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Then, no places, I think. Your dad's place, they're all he had in cultivation. It was right in the meadow. That's right. And all we had up home was a little patch right up at the house, and then maybe 20 acres over across the creek, 15 or 20 acres. But that is all. Now I go up there and I can see from the old home place right where I used to go to school. You can see clear across, so all the works.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: All those trees are gone, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It's all burned over. It burned over twice.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Oh, yeah?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. It burned over the first time in 1914, the second year we was here.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That makes it a bit scary. The big fire was 1931.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, that was the second fire. But the real big fire was in 1914. And Dad and Ed and them, they cut wood all winter long, cordwood. They'd just leave it out there laying there, and then they'd haul it in in the summer and pile it out. The next winter when Dad wasn't working, he'd haul it in here to the brickyard to sell it. That was our linen. Oh. Except thrashing. He'd go out thrashing. Well, Ed and him, and oh, I don't know, your dad was in that bunch too. They was all down there thrashing when this fire comes through, and I think it was 140 cords of wood we lost that year. But you talk about a fire, that was a fire. That second one was nothing compared to that one. It was a binger though. I know. I was here when it went through, out from down from the woods. But it was nothing compared to that other one. See, that was all green, solid timber. And he just took the whole work. So then the second one was dry. It was dry timber, but it wasn't air -stick.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you remember it as a child? What is that? Do you remember seeing the fire?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. We was out there all the time. We was carrying water. It took a blister to paint right off the side of the house. The house had been really painted. The side was just nothing but blisters.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The whole works.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It all had to be repainted, scraped and repainted again. I remember that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did the second fire burn the house?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No. They just took it down. It burned.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, but it didn't on that second fire because Davis was living there. And he was telling me how hard he worked. He used 35 gallons of water and 9 gallons of milk. Some cream. But he had it all figured out. What he took to save the house, he'd have to throw it on the roof when the sparks hit the shingles. So the house didn't burn then. When it burned, I don't remember. The barn burned. Yeah, that was it. The barn burned.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The barn burned.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The barn didn't burn the first time, but the second time.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you have any idea how these fires started?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The first fire.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was trying to think.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you remember them Tweedies?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It started over in there. I believe he was burning brush or stumps or something, and that's where the fire started. And then it just come right across and it come right through, and then through our place, shoemakers. And I believe it went there on over here to Spring Valley, and I don't remember how far, but it went through. But it just takes places here and there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Since it's all out in Big Nip?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: The second year, where did it start? The second year?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, it started over there and over west of the folks' place because we were there that day, and we saw this little spiral of smoke coming up. Mother and I walked up to the top of the hill to see where that smoke was, and it was farther over. It would have been over in the round of glass, so that's over there. I never did hear exactly, but I think that had been started in somebody's, I guess they figured that was somebody's fire too. But that was the heat of the day, my, in August.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I do know that Tweedy was blamed for that first fire that went through. Of course, it didn't make any difference. You couldn't do anything about it. Nowadays, it would, but then days it did.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's such a terrific whim with that second one. It just hooked it right through the treetops from hundreds of feet.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, it jumped. There used to be a road going across from the barn over to that meadow, you know, over where the dam is. And it jumped from that road. We thought they had it held there. But it jumped from there, clear across to our place up the shoemakers. Just that quick. It was about a mile jump in there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's what took the timber off the folks place across the creek. That was the fire that took theirs. It went right through there. It jumped from the side of the road and went right over into that timber.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It took the whole thing that cleared it.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did they have any kind of firefighting equipment in those days?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Just a bunch of people there with sacks and then fall trees and try to widen the fire break.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But you couldn't do nothing.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There was no sign of any fire equipment. Just wet sacks. Now we didn't have much water. We had to haul the water from the creeks. So there was no chance at all.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You did save the house. Oh yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We didn't have to use any milk either. Well, what Davis was telling me how hard you were. And he was telling somebody else, he says, when I told Mel how much I had to work and all I used, he said he just laughed at me. We'd have been better off if he got to get chirped. Oh, you were renting the house then. Well, the place was rented out, yeah. Oh, I see. So it was better off, turned around and sold. Traded the place afterwards. They got just as much for it anyhow.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I bet you had nightmares for a while. Oh yeah. Flames coming through your bedroom or something.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you start school out there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You know then?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I started school.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I went to the Big Mountain School. I started in 1906 that fall. We had, what do we figure, about two and a half miles to hike the school. Then in the wintertime, of course, when it's crested, we had our sleds and skis, so that was fun. We'd go right over the top of the snow up there, the long hills. Get up there and go down to the kid brother. He was on a sled, a brand new coat. The first time he had it on, Mackinac they called him. He was laying down in the sled and the wire fence coming. He couldn't stop. He went under and just ripped the coat right from the collar right down. Oh!

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You were scared to go home. Yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was he alright though?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh yeah, he didn't get it. He just ripped the coat. Yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I guess the coat was a pretty expensive item, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well it was. It didn't have much money, you know. It was just going along on cream money and butter and stuff that mothers sell.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I've seen that with twelve children in their family.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. That's what he was telling me earlier. But I believe we live just as good. We live better than a lot of them live right now. We always had, there was always plenty to eat. But we raised everything. Everything was raised right under the plates. We had six cows. Mother used to, two cans, ten gallons of cream a week she'd sell. And I think it was, I believe it was thirteen dollars a week she got in off of that. And my fat is in good shape. That time, you know, it didn't take too much.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Where did she sell it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They used to have a creamery down here. And that creamery, now, see it's, the creamery used to stand well where the park is down there. Right next corner there used to be a street going over to the warehouse. It stood right in the corner there. That a creamery. I believe they made butter and everything there if I remember right. I don't know if they had it right.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I know I worked there when they had the creamery down there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Oh did you?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Took samples of cream. I was trying to think. Farmers would bring in the cans of cream. We had a special little dip that we had to take it out of the stirrup and take a dip and put it in a little glass. Then you had to put it in the old running around to see what the butter fat was. Now they had a tester.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They had a little tester that they could tell how much butter fat there was.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you take the cream in to town for your mother then?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Once in a while. Not very often. I know my brother Vic did. Do you remember that Mrs.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh they used to live up on Putman Place. Then they moved because she was a great big woman. She was going to town and stopped in and one and always going to town. She had to walk. No she started down the road. And Vic was going to town with the cream. The buggy was side springs in. Oh there was soft springs. Nice riding buggy. He gets down the road. Stopped. She's flagging down. She wanted to ride. Ok. She gets in. She grabs a hold of the buggy and steps on the step to get up. The buggy just leaned over that way. Over went the cream cans. Spilled every step of the cream into the road. Oh no! There was another boy who was scared to come home.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well there was six, seven dollars worth of cream. That was the groceries for the week.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He never stopped to give that woman a ride again. I can't think of what her name was.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Bet she didn't ask again either. I hope not.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Bert Rutter used to stay over there. Bert Rutter used to stay over there with him. They lived in Bert Rutter's place. A nice little picture there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: It wasn't Runyon was it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did Runyon's live out there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No. It's another place. They lived on Randall plant. They lived on Randall plant. It was only about a mile and a half or two miles over there. It was a different road going in.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: McGregor.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: McGregor was the fella's name. Something like that. Gregory. That was ya.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I remember that one.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Gregory that served us. Oh she was a big wolf. She must have weighed two hundred and something.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: With all you boys afraid to go home makes me wonder what your mother was like.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, we was raced. We had our things to do if we didn't do it. While we were punished for it, it was the best thing in the world. That's where kids are spoiled today. We had our work to do. We had to do it and do it right. And if we didn't, well we didn't get too bad. We did a weapon. She had a little sweatshirt, but we'd remember the next time. And it didn't hurt us a bit. That's the best thing in the world. I'll tell you, if you've got twelve kids to race, you've got to do something. You let them run wild and you've got something. Oh no, we had a good life. I don't think there was anybody who had a better life than we had. We always had something to do. Archie and I play horseshoe. We'd be playing horseshoe all day long and go in and eat dinner. We'd jump up and start out and dad would say to Ed, Come on Ed, let's go see the fight. As soon as we get done playing horseshoe, if I won, we had to fight. See, whoever won the fight won the game.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Everything would go fine. We'd be a little argument about which shoe was the closest to the peg. Then if I'd win, he'd say, well now, that there shoe was back there. That belonged to me and I'd have won the game. No, none to fight it beyond. If he won, then I would say this. We had a fight every game.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: So it was never a dull moment.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I'll bet, your foremother.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, what were some of your chores that you had to do?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, we had to, in the summertime, we always had to go out after the cattle, morning and night. We'd have to get up about five in the morning, go out to get the cows and bring them in. Then we'd always trap and squirrel. We got a penny of squirrel, forever squirrel.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I'll get that too.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, from the time I was eight, nine years old, Archie and I were brothers. We'd cut a lot of wood for the house in the summertime. We'd split wood, carry in the wood. We had to do the dishes. I thought it was the morning's job before we went to school. It was the dishes. Well, the girls didn't have to do that. Well, there wasn't the sisters. Just one sister, younger me, she was two years younger. She was too young. And the rest were gone. I see. But Archie and I, we got smart one morning. We had the certain times that we had to leave the school and we'd make it. Oh, we'd play, surround.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: All at once, the mother says, the dish is done?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, we haven't got time. We've got to go to school. She pointed at the dishes, and if you're late to school, she said, you've had it. We got them dishes done and we run every step of the school. We never fooled her around after that. The next morning, them dishes were done. Well, to see her point, if she'd let us get by with them, that would have happened every day.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Which is what you had in mind, right?

words: Must be the two older ones that went gunning afterwards.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, there was three. I noticed the older ones that went gunning. Alfred, I don't believe, was one of them. All there was quite a time around here for a bit. But I pretty never forgot about that.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I see some of the...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: This is the town up here?

words: We never got up there very often, just if we played ball or something else. So we were kids. We didn't get in there at all.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did a lot of baseball play in there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Every Sunday. We wouldn't have a full team, and it would only be five or six, but we'd take on anybody in the country. For a while we did. There was four Fergusons and four Carlsons, and I forget who made up the rest of the team. But we only take one more. But then Bob and Lester were gone, and Ralph and Dick. So we just left Archie and I, and Clarence and Vernon. There was four of us.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: George King was up there, and then he'd be a player. There was five. There'd be five or six of us. Then we'd hike down.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You know where Spring Valley is?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Hool is there. I forget who was on the Spring Valley team, but he used to play in the Medd right out there. They'd only have four, five, six, whatever you can get. That made the deep. We had ball games every Sunday. Then they'd come up there, and we'd play down here. This field down here was going at that time. We'd come in here. We'd maybe get round the flats, or not round the flats, but we'd get some of Spring Valley and get a full team. Then we'd come down here and challenge somebody else. Didn't make no difference who won. I don't know if we ever won a game. We had a lot of fun.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was this something you organized ahead of time, or you just showed up?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, we'd get together and plan it to a certain extent. We never practiced. The only time we ever practiced ball was going to school. I know one practice of ball got me right there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I heard a little bit, you know what I'm saying?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But it hooked me in the foot. It might have knocked me out. It was right in the hands, but it was all right. You said the ball games were between the married men and the single men, and the girls didn't play. All the girls didn't play.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We used to have Fourth of July picnics up there quite a bit, and it was always the single men against the married men. Here was old Mulliken. He played professional baseball, and they had another one. I forget who it was that played professional baseball. We used to beat them. We had the best of them.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You were the single men. We could just run a little faster.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But in the wintertime, then, we used to have our coasting parties, skiing parties. We had a lot of fun there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh! It'll get hot. You just sat in the ball of the shovel. Just sat in the gym, get her feet up here. Oh! But that scoop is a shovel.

words: If you go far enough, it'll get hot.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Uh -huh. You don't know that.

words: Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, it'd be a foot to 18 inches. It just depends on if they kept the snow all off from us, so it'd freeze down good. Then they'd take crosscut sauce and just saw blocks. They'd just make a long cut and cut them crossways and have ice on them.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Crosscut sauce?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I like to use for a tree. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Same thing as you use for a tree. They wasn't lucky enough to have a chainsaw, then, so you could just go down. They had this old crosscuts. I know I hauled ice for them. They had to get the sawdust. Yeah, they'd take our sleds, and we'd just set the blocks in there and get a load, and then we'd haul it down, and Christie's down here. They had a big ice house, what they called an ice house. They'd pack the ice in there, then they'd cover it up with a foot or so.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Okay. I'm running. Now, bury the... I'm only talked up.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh.

words: And then...

words: Did we hang our creamer for the house? They sometimes hung it in the well, didn't they?

words: Yeah. But we never had a well. We never had a well. I don't know what we did. I know the folks had that cellar though. We had a cellar at home that was real cool.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You know, they'd have a foot of sawdust around, and then inside, it'd have three walls that'd have a four -inch airspace. And it'd never freeze in the winter, and it'd keep nice and cool all summer long. That's the way we kept our stuff there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Wouldn't that be vegetables and potatoes and things?

Must have been the other place that we were in before we came to Troy.

speaker: Karen Purtee

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Good student.

words: Naturally, you wouldn't expect me to say anything else.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You got all your lessons done right on time. Oh, yeah, yeah.

words: Had your favorite subject?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I'll tell you with me, I went out to Burnt Ridge, and I was supposed to be in the third grade. That's the reason I got into trouble out there. The teacher didn't have any third graders, so she put me back in the second grade. Well, I didn't have to study, because I knew everything. And then I goes back up to Big Meadows. I was out there one year and went back up to Big Meadows, and Edith Fogelstrom, she was in my class, she was in the fourth grade, and here I was in the third. I just skipped the third grade and goes into fourth grade. Didn't make any difference. It was always easy. Didn't bother me a bit.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You just juggled you around wherever you fit best, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Wherever I thought I could make it, I'd just tell them that's where I belong. The teacher wouldn't know any difference. I wouldn't have lost my report card, you know.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Oh, you'd lose your report card?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I lost my report card.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Conveniently.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, I skipped. That way a time or two. I got through. See, I wasn't fourteen yet when I got through the eighth grade. I was fourteen that fall, I was thirteen in the spring. And then I was in the eighth grade two years. Two years.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was doing all right. Kids were putting classes for convenience to the teacher. To the teacher, yeah. So if she only had one student in one grade, she'd move them around. That's the way I skipped the seventh grade, but I hadn't spent two years in the eighth grade.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What kind of jobs did you do then when you got out of school?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, old beans. Summertime, eight years old. I was out holding beans, making a dollar and a half a day. You talk about a proud boy, I made twenty -eight dollars. And we got to keep the money. The folks didn't take it. They took it. Well, then before school started, they had a... got a big hack.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You know what a hack is?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It's a... kind of a buggy, but it had two seats in it. They always called in a hack. It didn't have no topper, but I had a topper that had been a surrey. Oh, okay. So, uh... well, then they'd hook a team to those. I know Ed took his stuff one time to law school. We all got our money then. We went in, we picked out our own clothes, bought our own school clothes. So we were pretty proud boys. If we wanted to come into the show, we'd get a dime. They'd give us a dime that didn't come out of our money. It was an echo for the show and an echo for change. But of course we chipped in and bought cigarettes. Yeah! Oh! I'm sorry, George K. Archida. I think it was 15 cents for a pack of old -back cigarettes. I think there was 10 cigarettes in there. And we'd smoke them on the way home. Couldn't have them after we got home, so... We were sure they were smoked before we got there. But I know my first year is because I made 28 dollars. But I bought a suit, shirts, shoes. All the clothes I needed. I even bought a winter coat without 28 dollars. A pair of these knickerbocker pants.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh!

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Above the knee or below?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, right up here in the knee. They hung down. You remember what they were like. I'll tell you, I was proud. Blonde socks. Well, I think that's what I was wearing in the family picture. Yeah, your words. Something like it. It probably wasn't that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Why is that family picture?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, I know you were. Archida, you wanted to know.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: See, I don't know. It wouldn't have been that same suit, but it's one like it is. Because I was 12 then. I know when I was eight. It was the first year that I was up. But we bought all our own school clothes after that, with the money. And then from the time I was 13, I would go out with the whole, well, 12. When I was 12. That was the war I was on, and they couldn't get enough men. I was out here in the ridge shocking beans and hauling beans. We'd pitch on our own loads and then go in and have to pitch them off. We were working right with the men, load for load all the time. But I was pretty good sized. See, when I was 15, I weighed pretty near 200, about as much as I do right now. And that wasn't that. I was just in good shape. Working hard. I was 62 when I was 15 years old. Wow. Yeah, I've got these up there too.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Let's see.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: In Nelson and Troy took the picture. This is Archie, they all did. Great. This is the little girl that was burned. And her youngest one was burned today. And she was five. Oh, cool.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, that was the family picture there. And it was taken. They all got home. I see one Navy. Bill was in the Navy. He'd left. Ella had left. And I believe the rest of us, Mabel was home yet. And they'd left on Sunday. And her clothes cut fire, I believe, Monday morning. She died one night. It was three days after the picture. They didn't think she was burned bad. We didn't have any doctor here. They didn't think she was burned bad at all. But what had happened, she didn't hail the flames. And got infection. Oh, she was fine. Dax came home from town on Wednesday night. Had a watermelon. She loved watermelons. She sat down and ate a big meal. Ate a lot of watermelon. And that was about, well, that would be about six. We always ate at six. Supper was at six o 'clock sharp. And eight o 'clock, she started throwing spasms. I don't think she lasted only about an hour and a half. Not a day. That was 1980.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You said they didn't have a doctor here in town?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There was no doctor here in town. There was a young fellow. He'd had doctor training.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you remember him?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I can't think of his name. He had doctor training and everything, but he wasn't a doctor. And he said he wasn't a doctor.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: But you remember when that flu went around?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He was around to all of them, and he saved a lot of them too. He was good. And that night when she was throwing the spasms, he called Dr. Gritman in Moscow. Told him what he'd done. And Dr. Gritman says, there's nothing more you can do. He was bringing her up here. He said, you've done all you can do. So he was good. He'd had training, but he wasn't a doctor. He wasn't a full -fledged doctor. He was probably like one of these medics just now, you know. Similar to that. He had a lot of training, and he knew drugs and everything. And I can't think of that fellow's name, but he saved a lot of them here during the flu.

words: What kind of treatment did they have for the people that had flu?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't remember. I remember Mother was up there in Cuba, and they told me what they did. But I wasn't.

words: I believe it was through the winter, quite a bit.

words: I think that was a great school.

words: Something.

words: Well, you can't see. You're hidden.

words: I just can't heart someone for 12.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson

words: Yeah, but the tie is crossways like it always is, aren't you?

words: I can never keep a tie stretch. For the simple reason, I don't like them. I can't stand the shirt button.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I go around up there in the wintertime and people go, Why don't you button your shirt?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I said, Because then I freeze. I got out of my shirt open to get air down the circulates. Oh, this is up in Alaska. Keep an arm. All right. Okay.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Then they want to know why I don't wear a coat. I go all winter without a coat. But I always got one in the pickup case I needed. I'll just run around. Don't even wear heavy underwear. If I do wear a coat, it'll probably be, well, I've got it in there right now. It's a light red shirt, you know, that is. Of course, if I'm going out and just fooling around and things, I don't have a coat. But if I go to town, I just got that little light red coat, not a heavy one on the average.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, we got a thrashing crew here. Oh, yeah. That's late with the girls and the cook wagon. That was the sister. That was my two sisters. Oh, that was the sister. The two that was here.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Field and Mabel.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that's a pretty big crew. Oh, yeah.
