words: Where did you thrash? All around?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, they used to be down to the Genesee country. And then they would work their way up this way.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: See, Genesee was always earlier than this.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And the ringwelks. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, they'd start way down and start coming this way.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you work with the thrashing crew?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Not down there. I worked out on the ridge here for Walt Smith. Well, I worked for the Burn Creek Machine, So -and -Sachs, Ed and I, So -and -Sachs, all the time. Always the hardest job. They had to always pick the hardest job that it worked.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was that a hardest job? Was it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: So -and -Sachs, too. I was on a 32 -inch machine. And them Sachs had to be full. We'd jiggle them. And I started figuring one day. We put out, well, we was averaging close to around 700 when I was working for Walt Smith.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: A day?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, yeah. I was a small machine, and I was sewing them alone.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: 700 sacks a day?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But, uh... I was figuring 600. Then you jig them, Sachs. You jig them. You'd work them while they were on the Sacher. You'd take them off, and then you'd jig them. It was an average, I think, of seven times. You'd lift them, Sachs, set them down. Then you'd shake them, sit down and sew them. Then you'd pick them up, put them on your knee, and then carry them over to the Sachs pile. And the Sachs was averaging. Clarence Johnson told me when I was out there, he knows that last time I was down. He said them Sachs averaged pretty now 150 pounds apiece. Well, there's lifting them Sachs, each Sach eight times. Well, if it was 100 Sachs, there'd be 8 ,000 pounds. Multiply that by six, it'd be 48 ,000 pounds. You're lifting that a day, and you're carrying them one time. You think we'll talk about words. Yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Then what'd you do, pile them on a wagon or in a barn?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, side pile. They'd start at the ground, and then they'd be up five or high.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And then you just left them for the farmers?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, the farmers would take care of them.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But we'd have some big Sachs piles. They got rid of that, so now they just bulk it, dump it into grain grains. But this was all Sachs. And I've seen the time when Walt put out 800 Sachs a day. And I sold pretty much every Sach, but myself. It was always the same way. That Sach had to be so full. Yeah. A farmer would be there, be jiggly. If you missed a pound or two, every 150 Sachs would be an extra Sach you'd put out. And that Sach could maybe be five, ten cents. So you had to fill it.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. Look, they charged the farmer by the Sachs.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The farmer'd buy the Sachs. Then he had to... He paid so much a Sach, with ration to grain. So you see, you'd have to pay for the Sach. After Sach, you didn't get a full. And then it'd be that much more for the Sachs. That's the way that all works. They wanted them Sachs full.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So they were watching you, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, I fixed them. They'd bring old Sachs out, and they'd wanted them full. Get them full and start busting Sachs, and then they'd come and fill us in.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was another thing that the farmers would all do. But at harvesting time, they'd go and gather all the Sachs, let them in, get them ready. They had holes in the mice and two holes in them or something, and then they'd hatch them. So, well, you'd go by most any house in the fall when they were getting ready for harvest. Here they were, had plials of Sachs going through, getting the Sachs ready for harvest. Gunny Sachs.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And then they'd patch them with a Sach needle and, well, it was a crooked needle, Sachtoin. And then they got to use them flower paste, and they'd paste a Pach on. We never did. We sold ours. Well, yeah, but they started using that. And then a lot of the Sachs used two or three times. There'd be holes all around the top. And some of the women put it to these, but you couldn't run a needle through them. Oh, you talk about cows. We'd throw them over to the side and then the farmer'd get mad because we wouldn't, so we couldn't put a Sach needle through that. See, it would be stripped that wide. Well, then they'd want us to use them. So we'd let the Sach down and let them over. But you still couldn't do a decent job of sowing. But they'd put it in paste and flower.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: They were?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, perhaps the Sach would. We couldn't sow them.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, that is a good way.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What does a needle look like?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, you know what a spear looks like. Well, they'd be that, but the front part of it would be about that long. Everybody used a different length needle. Four or five inches long. But the needle would fit. Well, then the point would generally be about that long tapered, just like a spear. Then it'd come back the same way and then the shank would be, oh, maybe about as big around as, a little bit bigger maybe than that. And then the top of the pen. And then on the side it had a notch. So it would just bring the twine. All you'd do, you'd just hook it like that and thread it, see. Oh. You'd have a double twine.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I see. Kind of like a crochet hook almost. Well, no.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And then you'd pull through. It's entirely different.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Double?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Then the needle, you'd hit it right, the heel of it right there in your hand and you'd grab it. That's the way you judge the length of your needle. Oh. And you'd grab it right in the white part. And you'd shove it through. You'd shove it through with this, grab it in the other side. The palm of your hand and pull it out with your fingers. You'd do that three times and then you'd pull it tight.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And what was it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Nine stitches. Nine stitches in a sack of oats and eleven in a sack of wheat.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was standard. And it would length. And the needle was flat. Ah. The needle on the end was kind of flat.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, it's like a spear. Shaped like a spear. You know how a spear is. It's a four -sided. And then that's sacked. So one seam laid right over the other. Then you just sew that seam. And you just whipped it around the ground. It looked like a sewing machine. And you take your nice, pressed tie up and pull it off the needle. There's an ear on each side. You make a flip on it. I can still sew a sack, but my fingers are pretty crippled. I don't think I could do it very fast. But that, I always enjoyed it. I always enjoyed it. I'd be doing anything. That time of the year it would come out.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You gonna sew for me?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yep. Packed up and away I'd go.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You had a good time going with him, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Good food? What? Good food?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Them farmers out there, we boarded with the farmers. And the first farmer we'd go to work for, we'd start telling them how good the food was. It wouldn't make no difference what it was. It was always good though. It was a whole bunch of sweets. Then when we get to the next place, we'd tell them how good the food was back there. And each one had tried out do the other. I'll tell you, we laid high. And there's always chicken. Every place we got chicken, they'd go out and freshly kill chickens, you know. And that's pretty hard to beat. The store chicken, I don't care for that you go by. Yeah. And there's one woman, I forget, Mrs. Paul Rodin didn't have any chickens or something. But the boys go steal them. And I was trying to think, I think they wasn't stolen her own chickens.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: She was trying to save her chickens for herself?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: She didn't have any to spare her. Yeah, Walt and Elvin know about that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you know them, Walt Smith and Elvin Smith?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They can tell you about that. They get a big kick out of it. So you got your chickens after all anyway. Oh, we got the chicken people. We had the chicken people eat every place we went. But we'd always tell them what we got at the other place, you know. And then they'd try to beat it. But they had a hard time to beat it, Hilda's. Because they either always, well, he'd go and his uncle run a chicken farm. Then I'd read by all the roosters. They'd sell one day old that he'd go up there and he'd buy the roosters a penny apiece. He'd take them home and feed them, and right at harvest time they were just right for pride. But God, there'd be heaps of chicken on the table. But Hilda always had a good guard, so it was pretty hard to beat there. They were all good. They couldn't complain on any of them. Well, there was... there was Smiths and Westburgs and Kelbergs. You know, all of them, they were hard to beat. As you come down the line, every one of them was good cooks. There was just a big competition who was putting out the best feet, getting the best reputation.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And then you'd sleep out there, too?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah, I'd grab a straw pile, roll back a bunch of straw and throw down the bedding, go to sleep. If it rained, we'd pull the canvas, throw it up over the pace. And on one night I did that. It started raining, so I just covered myself up and went to sleep. And in the morning, I pulled the covers back, and there was a hole there, and I got all the water in my face. I woke up in the early morning. Someone slept under the wagons when they went to the Genesee. Yeah, the Genesee there, they always slept under the wagons there. Out here, when if it rained or something like that, we'd probably move into the barn or something like that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did that hold up your harvest in, didn't it, Ray?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Couldn't try to get to the guide out.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But it was good, happy to drive.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you have any run -ins with the, what was it, W -I -W -W?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, we never did. Not out here.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We did in the logging camps, but never in the harvest out around this one. No, in the logging camps, it's... I don't want to run into...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: 36, I think, was the big one here from after the first one in 1918, 1919, never close. We were in the 20s, and we had a big one strike up here. I just took the left of woods, went farming for a while. I didn't go back to the woods until 1940, I guess.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I worked for Walt Griscoops out here, and the richest, Joe and Jimmy's dad, did you know that? Do you saw with Brother Ed, too? What?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You saw with Brother Ed, too, Vincent. Oh, yeah, in the logging camp.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that's what I say. When you worked with him, you had to work. He had to be the top man. We'd go into a logging camp, we'd go to a song, his scale was the highest in the camp. They'd cut the price on us, had to work that much harder. Same amount of money. I tried to tell them, you know, didn't make any difference. They were going to make that money, and we got done with one strip. We saw it for two months, a little over. We started, and it was just a few days, and then they cut the price on us. We saw it that month, and they cut the price on us at the end of the month. Then when they got done with the strip, Oscar Sturgill says, I'll show you a dollar strip in the morning. I said, not me. Ed said, no, I said, I think we go harvesting. So we went harvesting. I think that's the last I saw of them.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: How did they pay you now?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: So much in the house.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They'd have a skater come around and scare their logs, and it was so much in the house. Then I went up to Elk River, and I started in Grady, building these roads by hand, number two shovels, bummer roads, that was dolly roads for Skid. Then come down to Camp 7, you remember. We needed another man, just Ollie and I. I come down, see Ed, and well, yeah, he'd come up. I told him, I think I told him I'd guarantee him $10 a day, and that was pretty good wages at that time. So he come up, and he went out and looked it over. He said, we'd make more money than that. I said, listen. I said, before, when I worked with you, you as the boss. This time I'm the boss. I've got this job. We're making $10 a day. Well, we worked that whole fall, winter, next summer, straight through $10 a day, and that is most anybody got. But if we had tried to make more, finally he said, well, it's just no use setting a price.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He said, how about if I just pay you $10 a day?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And that's the way we worked. When we get done, we measure up, and then he'd figure out so much a station that'd make the $10 a day. We'd do it so much 100 feet. Just worked, and we worked, but we didn't work as hard as we would have if we'd worked the other way. We got our prices cut. Well, that was one time I was the boss.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I think most of the boys worked with him, saw eight and a half little big. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They'd come rattle. They both saw it. Archie. Archie. Archie saw it wood with him. Cordless. Until he froze his toes, and then I had to go out. I don't know what it was. I had 14. I worked for a while, but that was enough. I had been working for Westburg. I had money. I didn't have to work that hard. I stayed in and done the cooking. Mother was sick that winter, so I stayed in and done the cooking. She'd sit there and tell me what to do.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And you learned how to cook.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, I learned how to cook. I learned how to bake bread. I made just the swellest bread you ever seen. And then she had to go back to the hospital.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So Dad says, can you make bread, kid?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah, you bet. I went ahead and made some bread. I forget what happened.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was storming real bad, so Dad was hauling wood, and I took the other team in the front by, but took the hours and pranked the stool around the road. Well, when I got back, the bread had set too long. So it was kind of sour. It looked good. It looked fine. It was sour. So Ed was going to make milk toast. I would have sour the milk.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, geez. That wasn't an awful sour bread. It was. Oh.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, then Dad said, well, don't give up. He said, try it again. He said, I know you made good bread when Mother was here. So I did. And I'd done everything exactly the same, I thought. And baked it. No, it was the most beautiful bread you ever seen. I went to cut it. This is so damn hard, I couldn't cut it. Ed said, I threw some out to the chickens. He said, and they broke their bills.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that ended the bread list, because I didn't have to bake any more bread.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you figure out what you did wrong?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, I baked bread since. I baked bread up there once in a while. They put me just cooking up there in the camp one spring.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I baked bread then, and it turned out fine. I don't know what I did wrong. I never have been able to figure it out.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They put me to cooking for the engineers, three weeks job. Well, I don't know whether I fed them so good they got done, or if they couldn't eat my cooking, but they done it in two weeks. No way!

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, you see, there's an idea, what kind of a cook I did. Oh, yeah, well, I'm afraid to say anything.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Don't bother me.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, see, you folks were thinking of some other games, too. Oh, that's what they played. Yeah, she's got the whole list of them. I got a list of games that we did. I hadn't even thought I'd forgot about them.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Ready?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Okay. Yeah, well, these were some that we had played a lot, and I'd even forgotten about it. That one, of course, to name them, Fight Red Rover and Flying Catchman and Run Sheep Run. Fox and Geese we played in the winter in the snow. We made a big ring out the snow and cut it like a pie and had the center was trumped out, and that's where they had to... that was the prison. Oh, they'd go catch around there. Kick the can, that was a favorite, and hide and seek. We played that, and no ghosts out tonight.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: No ghosts out?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, should have Melvin tell that one, but just exactly how to play that one. I kind of forgot that, but I do remember we played it a lot, and that's Andy Over. Andy, Andy Over. We played that constantly, I think. We had a softball, and they divided up the group. We had two sides, one would be on one side of the schoolhouse and the other on the other. We played it at home, too, in our houses, and they tossed the ball over the roof. Then you'd holler, Andy, Andy Over.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They tossed the ball over the schoolhouse, and the one on the other side would try to catch the ball. If they caught the ball, then they'd try to sneak around the schoolhouse to see if they could catch somebody with the ball. If they had it, they could, whoever they either touched with it or threw the ball and hit, had to stay with them then. Oh, and the one that had the most over on their side was the winner. We did it over and over. That was much fun. Then, of course, that pump pump, the pull away, we played that a lot. Then we had the last couple of hours, and they'd have to go down, probably for that one, and they played that one. They'd divide up two and two and two, and they'd line up in a straight line, and then they'd have someone be it, would stand out in front with their back turned to the roof. They'd haul our last couple out, and the last couple that was in the back of the line would run, and the object was to try and touch each other so they wouldn't get caught, but the one that was in front tried to catch one of them. It caught one, and the one that wasn't caught had to be it. They made a call. This was a boy and girl type one. If they happened to be the age, they wanted to be a boy and a girl together, otherwise, it's not the time for two girls and two boys. They abandoned how they felt about the girls and the boys. Then we had another one, the keep away. That was played with a ball, too, and then we had various types of tag games. We could have stone tag or base tag, wall tag, as long as you had it understood ahead of time. They couldn't tag you. They could have touched you. I have never played fruit basket upset. Well, there they would all sit in a circle, and each one would be given a name of some type of fruit, and the one that was it would stand out in the middle, and they'd say, like, peaches, pears. Well, the ones that had been named peaches and pears would exchange seats, and while they were exchanging seats, the one that was it would try to grab a seat, and if she succeeded, the one that didn't get to sit down would be it, and if the one that was up there quite a while and hadn't been able to get a seat, she'd say, fruit basket upset, and she'd be right sure that she was close to her chair and she'd get a seat. Well, then everybody had to move. Then everybody had to move, yeah, and that was so...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did everybody know what fruit everybody was?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, they would be named. So if you could remember what... who the banana was, then you could get close to that seat. You just remember that yourself. You know, if you were the banana, you'd remember you were the banana, so if they called banana away and they called banana, you were banana. You'd trade with whoever twice they named, and they'd say, suppose they said lemon. Well, then you'd watch to see who the lemon was, and when the lemon would lock to see who the banana was, they'd go and swap chairs, and the one that was it, meanwhile, had been named the name too, of course, and when they switched, she'd try to get one of those places. Oh, that is good. They had us doing that one, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And then... Just one way, I can't... Just remember, I'll have to ask you a little bit. Keep away. And then another thing, they did add a lot of... on the spin of the platter, you know what that is, I imagine. Well, they'd be sitting in a circle again, and somebody that was it would twist... take a platter or a plate, and they'd twist it like that, and they'd holler... I think they were named numbers. Seven, say, seven and four, or seven, and seven would try to dash out there and catch it before the plate landed on the floor. If they did, then the one that was it had to do it again and call another number. But if the plate landed flat on the floor, then the number that didn't get there and died to pick it up had to be it. Oh! That was spring and spin the platter. They had lots of... That wasn't like spin the bottle. That isn't like spin the bottle. On the same order, I suppose. Because then they'd twist the bottle. Yeah, but that's a kissing game. Oh! The way I played it. Yeah! No kissing game on spin the platter. Oh, okay. Oh! Kick the can, of course. They'd have a can out there, as I recall, and somebody would kick the can, and while the one that was it was going to rescue the can, everybody ran and hid. And so the times you'd get the can back there or he would put the can there while they had to start out like hide in the seat and find them. And I believe that if the can was there, somebody could dash in and kick the can again. And then they'd have to go on... The one that was it, they'd have to go and rescue the can again, and everybody that was in could run and hide again. Oh! That was lousy. I'd hate to be the poor fellow chasing the can, you know. Wow, right. And then this clap in, clap out, that was... They had double seats, you know. Somebody would come to sit by you while you clapped the can. They had to get out. Or if the clap in, I suppose you could clap your can and come there, but the one that was it would try to get the place from a good place. And they had these magic tricks. Did you ever... Did you know that if you had to have an accomplice, but of course it wouldn't always tell this, or yes, they did, an accomplice would go out of the room and while he was out, why somebody would have a broomstick and you would catch the broomsticks, either up at the top or the middle or down at the bottom. And then he'd say, ready, and the accomplice would come in and he would tell where the broomstick had been touched. And they couldn't figure out how they could tell. He'd tell by smelling. He'd pick the broomstick up and he'd smell them all from there and he'd smell the bottom.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Smell the broom.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was down the bottom. Sure enough it was. But they couldn't figure out how they could tell. How could that one that was outside, absolutely out of sight, how could he tell where they had touched the thing by smelling it. Everybody went around smelling the broomstick to see if it actually did smell different. Yes. They had a little secret, the one that was holding or was inside had it. Maybe he might have his two feet together, bottom. Or he might be reaching up there, scratching at your top. Or he might be holding his hands, middle, or whatever signal they had agreed with before. I see. And so all the accomplice had to look at and see what the physician was that the one inside had. And he could smell it. Boy, he could smell it. Oh, he could really smell it. I had quite a number of those. That was one of them. I remember that really had them guessing. And then three deep, that was, they had that, that was fun.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What is it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You know, they had to do that three deep. They'd have, again, two and two and two. But instead of standing side by each, they'd stand two deep. There'd be one in front and one behind, and then a big circle. Then the one that was it, or there'd be one chasing one. There'd have to be two. And this one would chase that one. And he'd run and stand in front of one of these couples. And then the one that was chasing would chase the one that was in the back. Oh, I see. And that one, then that one would have to run and find a place in front of somebody. And they'd just keep that up until they got rid of it. There was no ending, I guess. Yeah. Unless they caught, yeah. But then that was fun. And the musical chair was another one they enjoyed. And that one, you know, I'd play that. They had to double roll. And they'd have one chair or less, and there were players. And one that would be it. And he'd try to get a chair. And when the music played, everybody circled around and around and around. And when the music went, they sat down. And there'd always be one left out, because there's one chair too few. And the one that was it would try to be sure that he wasn't the one left out. And he'd try to get a chair while the music was going. Anyway, the one that was left out without a chair had to be hit. And then they'd keep going until they got all the chairs away. And then there'd just, or one chair. And there'd be two people circulating around that one chair. And when the music quit, the one that was the quickest got the chair. And the one that was left was lost.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Without. Yeah, without.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And then these singing ones we had, Skip to My Lou was one of the favorite ones. Little red wagon painted blue, little red wagon painted blue, little red wagon painted blue, skip to my darling. And we'd make up all kinds of things. It takes melons, it's tell, remember the things that we have to do. Now this is one of the things that's a game that it's all right to dance to. And I remember they gave these out in the middle. And we did those. They were fun. Your father did let you do them. We were permitted to do those. And they were fun. They were just lots of fun. And this Captain Jinx, the Martian Reams. That was one that can't quite remember all of those. So Virginia Reel, an old band tucker. And as Susan said, we were going to have to get together with some of these old runners around here to try and remember how they are played. Because we forgot. And Pop Goes the Weasel was another one that we did. And Go In and Out the Window. Go In and Out the Window. Now they had a big circle I remember. And they go in, go in and out the window, go in and out the window. Go in and out the window as you've done before. And then we can't remember the line. If they were to go and stand before someone, or just what they were supposed to do. This is one couple would go in and out between the other couples. Mm -hmm. And they would hold their arms out like that. So they would win that. And they would weave around. But I can't remember just exactly. That's what I say. We're going to have to. Because they were all fun. And as Pop Goes the Weasel, that was fun. An old ant tucker. That was another favorite.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: How did that go?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, that's the skull. That was a fun one. That's the skull. That took two couples. They would do that one. Two, four couples. Two here. Right. Two here and two here. Four. It's like a square dance. Four couples.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: A similar two square dance. Four, it's like four couples would do that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Two facing and two facing, right?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And then they go best of skull. Well, the best of skull of all the skulls. Best of skull. And then that makes the best of skull. That's the skull of all the skulls. That's the skull where it went.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And then they drop their partners and they go in and weave in and out, right?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was similar to a square dance. And then when they'd get through, they'd swing their partners. They'd grab and hold their arms, I think, and swing around. I don't know. So it'd be like a grand right and left. Yeah. They'd go dancing in and out.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Instead of grabbing hands, they'd hook elbows, I believe. Take cold, quick arms together. A square dance. I believe they'd grab... No, they have... They'd grab hands and the element left to match.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But then they had the other. They'd hook elbows.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, they went around from person to person. They'd hook the elbows instead of hands. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You see, they'd hook that way and then go around and then square dance. And the element left or that or the element right where they'd always use their hands to take their hands and sit and zigzag around back and forth.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Now, was your family allowed to dance?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. So that you did...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Besides the folk dances?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. They used to... Fred Johnson had a big pavilion there on the Paulson place.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Remember he bought that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. We used to go... I was a kid. I didn't do much dancing, but we'd be able to...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We'd dance.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I know Ralph and Big. Bill L. Dance.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't think that we did... We were a little bit in the younger set again, so we didn't get in on it because, like, those other kids were older. And we were the ones that really did more of it. But when we got on Burke Ridge, everybody did it out there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And that was really fun. Up there at that place, see, I was, what, around 12, 13 when we had that. So we wasn't doing any dancing.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We could go over and listen to the music.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: They had live music or the tools?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The buyer's bunch. There's some of their relations around here yet, but they played. And then we used to go clear over a little later, clear over to...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did they call that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was out of Moscow. They had a big hall over there. I forget what they called that hall. But we used to go over there, and the buyers played over there. They used to play all over, and the buyers were pretty good musicians.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But I know we played them out at Union School between here and Moscow, and then out at Burke Ridge, we did them and done them. These are the folk dances, though. Come on, I was a Lewis tonight. I learned what fun they were. We did it all the time for up to eight in there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's when I learned out what fun it was.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, what else you definitely missed there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Of course, we had that Virginia wheel. That one, they formed two lines facing each other, and then they come from opposite, like this one. I don't remember how it ended, but I know it was... But did they...all the schoolhouses had either an organ or piano.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Is that how they had the music?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I can't even remember. Oh, and Burke Ridge, you had a piano. You remember, Hilde used to play that. I couldn't remember who had played it, but I knew we had a piano out there. Yeah, Hilde played it most of the time. There were several others. But she got it big metal.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We didn't have anything up there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was wondering if you had any.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you have a phonograph?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, you used to take the phonograph over time. I think it was a phonograph used there, because I don't think it was a musical instrument. I remember Roy and Allison when he was singing that song, No Music. He had quite a time. He just began. Oh, these literaries.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We were going through some of the things, the programs that they did have, and he agreed that when the literaries, he was sure that they had them regularly at least twice a month.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I'm pretty sure they had them. I know they had them once a month, and I'm pretty sure that they had them twice a month.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, Helena and I had to go home every time they pushed the benches out of the way to dance.

speaker: Karen Purtee
